desc: 全题型 · 全章节覆盖

questions.text: A landlord collects a deposit of £1,800 from a new tenant whose monthly rent is £1,500 (annual rent £18,000). Which statement BEST describes the landlord's position?
opts: The deposit is within the permitted limit as it is less than two months' rent
The deposit exceeds the permitted cap under the Tenant Fees Act 2019 and must be reduced
The deposit is acceptable as there is no cap for monthly rents above £1,000
The deposit cap only applies at the start of the tenancy
optExpl: Incorrect — the cap is five or six weeks, not two months
Correct — annual rent £18,000 < £50,000, so cap is 5 weeks = £1,730.77; £1,800 exceeds this
Incorrect — no such exemption exists
Incorrect — the cap applies throughout
questions.expl: Annual rent £18,000 < £50,000 threshold → maximum deposit = five weeks' rent: £1,500×12÷52×5 = £1,730.77. The landlord has charged £1,800, exceeding the cap by £69.23.
questions.key: 🔑 Annual rent < £50,000 → 5 weeks max | ≥ £50,000 → 6 weeks max

questions.text: Indicate whether each circumstance would or would not prevent a landlord from serving a valid Section 21 notice.

rows.left: Deposit protected, but prescribed information not served on the tenant
Landlord has a valid EPC exemption registered on the PRS Exemptions Register
Tenant complained to the local authority about disrepair four months ago
Tenancy is currently within the fixed term — three months remaining
Landlord did not carry out a right to rent check before the tenancy began
Current Gas Safety Certificate expired two weeks ago

questions.expl:
• Prescribed information not served → prevents S21 even if deposit is protected
• Registered EPC exemption → does not prevent (compliant state)
• Complaint 4 months ago → prevents (retaliatory eviction, 6-month rule, Deregulation Act 2015)
• Fixed term still running → S21 cannot be served
• Right to rent not done → does NOT prevent S21 (separate obligation, not S21 precondition)
• Gas Safety Certificate expired → prevents S21
questions.key: ⚠️ Trap: Right to rent check failure does NOT invalidate Section 21
questions.ref: Housing Act 1988 s.21; Deregulation Act 2015 ss.33–41

questions.text: A tenant reports the boiler has stopped working in January. Under Section 11 of the Landlord and Tenant Act 1985, which obligations does the landlord have? (Select all that apply.)
opts: Repair the boiler within a reasonable timeframe after being notified
Provide the tenant with alternative heating while repair is arranged
Keep the installations for the supply of gas in repair and proper working order
Replace the boiler with a more energy-efficient model if it cannot be repaired
Reimburse the tenant for costs incurred due to lack of heating
optExpl: Correct — Section 11 requires repair within reasonable time after notification
Incorrect — providing temporary heating is good practice but not a s.11 statutory obligation
Correct — s.11 expressly includes gas supply installations
Incorrect — obligation is to repair, not upgrade
Incorrect — compensation for consequential loss is tort law, not s.11 directly
questions.expl: Section 11 LTA 1985 covers: (1) structure and exterior; (2) water, gas, electricity and sanitation installations; (3) space heating and hot water. Temporary heating (B) and upgrading (D) are not s.11 requirements.
questions.key: 🔑 S11 = Structure + Water/Gas/Elec + Heating/Hot water. Nothing more.

questions.text: Fill in the blanks to complete the statements about mandatory HMO licensing.

blanks.sentence: An HMO requires a mandatory licence when occupied by ___ or more persons forming ___ or more separate households.
opts: five
three
two
four

blanks.sentence: A landlord without a licence may face a civil penalty of up to ___. Tenants may apply to the FTT for a Rent Repayment Order of up to ___ months' rent.

questions.expl: Mandatory HMO licensing threshold: 5+ persons from 2+ separate households. Three-storey rule abolished in 2018. Civil penalty: up to £30,000. Rent Repayment Order: up to 12 months' rent.
questions.key: 🔑 5 persons / 2 households / £30,000 / 12-month RRO

questions.text: A landlord lets a property to a friend for six months at a reduced rent. The friend already owns their own home and will continue living there at weekends. Which tenancy type is MOST appropriate?
opts: An assured shorthold tenancy, as it is for a fixed term of six months
A contractual tenancy, as the property will not be the friend's only or principal home
A licence to occupy, as the arrangement is informal and between friends
A statutory periodic tenancy, as no fixed term has been formally agreed
optExpl: Incorrect — AST requires the property to be the tenant's only or principal home
Correct — not the principal home → Housing Act 1988 does not apply → contractual tenancy
Incorrect — the friend will have exclusive possession, making this a tenancy not a licence
Incorrect — statutory periodic arises when fixed term expires; cannot be created from scratch
questions.expl: An AST requires the dwelling to be the tenant's only or principal home (HA 1988, s.1). The friend continues to own and occupy their own home, so the condition is not met. Use a contractual tenancy.
questions.key: AST prerequisite: only or principal home. Not principal home = no AST.

questions.text: A tenant has failed to pay rent for the second consecutive month. Which actions are available to the landlord? (Select all that apply.)
opts: Serve a Section 8 notice using Ground 8
Serve a Section 8 notice using Grounds 10 and 11 alongside Ground 8
Change the locks immediately to recover the property
Serve a Section 21 notice as a backstop measure
Apply to court for a possession order without serving any notice first
optExpl: Correct — Ground 8 (mandatory) triggered at two months' arrears
Correct — Grounds 10 and 11 can be used alongside Ground 8
Incorrect — changing locks = illegal eviction, criminal offence
Correct — S21 as backstop is legitimate strategy
Incorrect — must serve notice and wait notice period before applying to court
questions.expl: At two months' arrears: Ground 8 (mandatory), Grounds 10 and 11 (discretionary), and Section 21 as backstop. Changing locks (C) = criminal offence under Protection from Eviction Act 1977.
questions.key: ⚠️ Changing locks = ALWAYS wrong. S8 Ground 8 + S21 in parallel = correct strategy.
questions.ref: Housing Act 1988, Schedule 2; Protection from Eviction Act 1977

questions.text: Indicate whether the following activities should take place at check-in, check-out, or both.

rows.left: Recording the condition of all rooms and contents in writing
Taking meter readings for gas, electricity and water
Asking the tenant to sign to confirm they agree with the written record
Noting any items that are missing or have been damaged during the tenancy
Handing over keys and any relevant documents to the tenant
Comparing the current condition of the property against the original record

questions.expl:
• Condition record and meter readings: both (establish and close baseline)
• Tenant signature: check-in (confirms initial condition)
• Missing/damaged items: check-out (compared against inventory)
• Handing over keys: check-in only
• Comparing against original record: only possible at check-out
questions.key: Check-in = establish baseline. Check-out = compare against baseline.
questions.ref: ARLA Propertymark guidance; TDS adjudication principles

questions.text: A landlord provides a Gas Safety Certificate, EPC and EICR at tenancy start but doesn't get the tenant's signature. Six months later, they wish to serve Section 21. Which BEST describes the position?
opts: The landlord can serve Section 21 as all required documents were provided
The landlord may struggle to prove documents were received without a signature
The landlord cannot serve Section 21 because the 'How to Rent' guide was not provided
The landlord must re-provide the documents and get a signature first
optExpl: Incorrect — the list appears complete but the How to Rent guide is missing
Partially true but not the primary issue
Correct — the How to Rent guide is a prescribed document; without it S21 cannot be validly served
Incorrect — re-providing documents doesn't remedy the missing How to Rent guide
questions.expl: Classic exam trap: three documents listed → looks complete. The 'How to Rent' guide is the critical missing prescribed document. Without it, Section 21 is invalid.
questions.key: ⚠️ Trap: 3 docs listed = looks complete. The 4th (How to Rent guide) is always the one they omit.
questions.ref: Assured Shorthold Tenancy Notices and Prescribed Requirements (England) Regulations 2015

questions.text: Fill in the blanks about increasing rent on a statutory periodic tenancy.

blanks.sentence: A landlord must serve a ___ notice, giving at least ___ month(s) notice before the increase takes effect.
opts: Section 13
Section 8
one
two

blanks.sentence: A tenant who disagrees may apply to the ___ within the notice period. If the tenant pays the new rent ___, they lose the right to challenge it.
opts: First-tier Tribunal
County Court
voluntarily
under protest

questions.ans: Section 13 / one month / First-tier Tribunal / voluntarily
questions.expl:
• Section 13 notice: statutory mechanism for rent increase on periodic tenancy
• One month's notice for monthly tenancies
• First-tier Tribunal: tenant's forum to challenge excessive increase
• Voluntary payment: paying new rent = acceptance = no FTT challenge
questions.key: 🔑 Paid voluntarily = accepted = FTT challenge right lost.

questions.text: Indicate whether each repair is the landlord's or the tenant's responsibility.

rows.left: Repairing a crack in the external wall caused by subsidence
Replacing a shower curtain that has become mouldy through normal use
Unblocking a drain that the tenant has blocked with cooking fat
Repairing a communal door entry system that has stopped working
Replacing a smoke alarm battery that has run out
Repairing internal door damage caused by the tenant's dog

questions.expl:
• Subsidence crack (external wall): structural → landlord (s.11)
• Mouldy shower curtain (normal use): tenant's day-to-day maintenance
• Drain blocked by fat: tenant caused it → tenant (tenant-like use)
• Communal door entry: shared installation → landlord
• Smoke alarm battery: small consumable → tenant
• Dog damage: pet damage → tenant
questions.key: Landlord = structure, exterior, shared systems. Tenant = consumables, self-caused and pet damage.

questions.text: A Grade II listed property built in 1890 cannot be improved beyond EPC rating F. Which BEST describes the landlord's options?
opts: Must bring the property to E rating before letting, regardless of listed status
May register an exemption on the PRS Exemptions Register and let lawfully while valid
Listed buildings are automatically exempt from EPC requirements
Must obtain planning permission before carrying out works to improve the rating
optExpl: Incorrect — landlords genuinely unable to reach E may register a valid exemption
Correct — where improvement is infeasible, a cost cap or other exemption can be registered on the PRS Exemptions Register
Incorrect — listed buildings are NOT automatically exempt; exemption must be actively registered
Incorrect — planning permission is separate from registering an MEES exemption
questions.expl: Where a landlord has spent up to £3,500 and still cannot reach E — or improvements would alter a listed building's character — they may register a cost cap exemption on the PRS Exemptions Register (lasts 5 years). Listed status does not create automatic exemption.
questions.key: ⚠️ Listed building ≠ automatic exemption. Must actively register on PRS Exemptions Register.

questions.text: Which information would a landlord MOST reasonably expect in a letting agent's Terms of Business? (Select all that apply.)
opts: A clear description of the services the agent will provide
The fees payable by the landlord and when they become due
The name and qualifications of every member of staff
The landlord's legal obligations regarding safety at the property
The agent's complaints procedure and redress scheme membership
optExpl: Correct — services description is a core requirement
Correct — fees and payment terms must be clearly stated
Incorrect — individual staff names and qualifications not required
Correct — landlord safety obligations must be included
Correct — redress scheme membership must be disclosed by law
questions.expl: ToB must include: (A) services; (B) fees and timing; (D) landlord safety obligations; (E) complaints procedure and redress scheme. Individual staff qualifications (C) are not required.
questions.key: ToB must include: Services / Fees / Safety obligations / Complaints & redress
questions.ref: Consumer Rights Act 2015; Letting Agents (Transparency of Fees) Regulations 2015

questions.text: Fill in the blanks about the consequences of failing to protect a deposit.

blanks.sentence: A landlord must protect the deposit within ___ days. The court may order a penalty of between ___ and three times the deposit amount.
opts: one
two

blanks.sentence: The landlord cannot serve a valid ___ notice until the deposit is protected and prescribed information served. A custodial scheme must return the deposit within ___ days of agreement.
opts: Section 21
Section 8

questions.expl:
• 30-day deadline to protect and serve prescribed information
• 1 to 3 times deposit as penalty (court discretion)
• Section 21 blocked — Section 8 is not affected
• 10 days: custodial scheme must return deposit once parties agree
questions.key: 🔑 30 days → 1–3× penalty → blocks S21 (not S8) → 10 days return

questions.text: Indicate whether the following checks are required by law, best practice, or not appropriate.

rows.left: Right to rent document check
Credit reference check
Previous landlord reference
Medical history check
Affordability / income assessment
Criminal record (disclosure) check

questions.ans: Required: Right to rent | Best practice: Credit / Previous landlord / Affordability | Not appropriate: Medical / Criminal
questions.expl:
• Right to rent: legally required (Immigration Act 2014)
• Credit, previous landlord, affordability: not legally required but strongly recommended
• Medical and criminal record: could constitute discrimination under Equality Act 2010
questions.key: Right to rent = ONLY legally required check. Medical and criminal checks can be discriminatory.

questions.text: A property is occupied by five people: two sisters, one sister's husband, a family friend, and an unrelated lodger. Does this property require a mandatory HMO licence?
opts: No — because some occupiers are family members
Yes — five or more persons forming two or more separate households
Only if the property is on three or more storeys
It needs a selective licence but not a mandatory HMO licence
optExpl: Incorrect — not all five form a single household; family friend and unrelated lodger are separate households
Correct — 5 persons from 3 households clearly meets the mandatory threshold
Incorrect — the three-storey requirement was abolished in 2018
Incorrect — this meets mandatory, not just selective, licensing criteria
questions.expl: Households: (1) two sisters + husband; (2) family friend; (3) unrelated lodger. Total: 5 persons, 3 households → mandatory HMO licence required.
questions.key: Count households carefully. Friends and unrelated lodgers are separate households.
questions.ref: Housing Act 2004, s.55; HMO licensing guidance 2018

questions.text: Indicate whether the following safety requirements are mandatory under legislation, required under authoritative guidance, or neither.

rows.left: Annual gas safety check and record by a Gas Safe registered engineer
Electrical installation condition report (EICR) every five years
Legionella risk assessment
Condensation risk assessment document
Furniture meeting the Furniture and Furnishings (Fire Safety) Regulations 1988
Annual roof inspection by a qualified surveyor

questions.ans: Mandatory: Gas/EICR/Furniture | Guidance: Legionella | Neither: Condensation/Roof
questions.expl:
• Gas Safety / EICR / Furniture: statutory obligations
• Legionella: HSE ACoP L8 — authoritative guidance, must follow
• Condensation risk assessment: no such prescribed document
• Annual roof inspection: no legal requirement
questions.key: Legionella = HSE guidance (not statute). Gas + EICR + Furniture = statutory.
questions.ref: Gas Safety Regulations 1998; Electrical Safety Standards PRS 2020; Furniture Regs 1988; HSE ACoP L8

questions.text: An AST fixed term has just expired and the tenant wishes to stay. Which options are valid for the landlord? (Select all that apply.)
opts: Do nothing — the tenancy becomes a statutory periodic tenancy automatically
Sign a new fixed-term tenancy agreement
Require the tenant to vacate immediately as the fixed term has ended
Allow the tenancy to continue as a contractual periodic tenancy if the original agreement provides for this
Issue a Section 21 notice now that the fixed term has expired
optExpl: Correct — s.5 HA 1988: automatic statutory periodic on expiry
Correct — both parties may agree a new fixed term
Incorrect — expiry does not automatically end the tenant's right to occupy; legal process required
Correct — if the original tenancy contained a contractual continuation clause
Correct — S21 can be served at end of fixed term or during periodic tenancy
questions.expl: Fixed term expiry does not end the tenant's right to occupy. Four valid options: (A) let roll into statutory periodic; (B) new fixed term; (D) contractual periodic if provided for; (E) serve S21 notice.
questions.key: Fixed term expiry ≠ tenancy ends. S21 takes effect at end of notice period, not immediately.
questions.ref: Housing Act 1988, s.5; Protection from Eviction Act 1977

questions.text: Fill in the blanks about factors affecting achievable rental value.

blanks.sentence: When carrying out a market appraisal, an agent will consider the property's ___ as the primary factor affecting rental value. The condition of the ___ will also significantly influence the rent achievable.
opts: location
size
decoration and fixtures
garden

blanks.sentence: Properties near good transport links and schools tend to appeal to a ___ market. An agent should use ___ from similar properties to support their rental recommendation.
opts: family
student
comparables
their own judgement

questions.ans: location / decoration and fixtures / family / comparables
questions.expl:
• Location: single most important driver of rental value
• Decoration and fixtures: directly affects achievable rent
• Family market: typical for well-connected properties with space
• Comparables: professional standard for supporting valuation
questions.key: Market appraisal = Location + Condition + Target market + Comparables.

questions.text: A tenancy agreement requires the tenant to have carpets professionally cleaned at the end of every tenancy, regardless of their condition. The tenant challenges this. Which BEST describes the legal position?
opts: The clause is valid because both parties freely signed the agreement
The clause is likely unenforceable as it requires the property to be returned in a better condition than received
The clause is enforceable as professional carpet cleaning is reasonable
The clause can only be challenged if the tenant proves the carpets were dirty at the start
optExpl: Incorrect — Consumer Rights Act 2015 can override freely signed unfair terms
Correct — requiring cleaning 'regardless of condition' = returning in better state than received = significant imbalance = unfair term
Incorrect — cleaning in principle is reasonable; 'regardless of condition' makes it unfair
Incorrect — the unfairness is in the clause's absolute nature, not the initial condition
questions.expl: A clause requiring cleaning 'regardless of condition' effectively requires the tenant to return the property in a better state than received, creating a significant imbalance — likely an unfair term under Consumer Rights Act 2015.
questions.key: Mandatory cleaning 'regardless of condition' = unfair term.

questions.text: Before marketing a property for let, which of the following must a landlord have in place? (Select all that apply.)
opts: A valid EPC rated E or above (or a registered exemption)
Written consent from their mortgage lender
A current gas safety record if the property has gas appliances
A selective licence if the property is in a designated licensing area
An EICR dated within the last five years
optExpl: Correct — EPC at E or above is required before a property can be let or marketed
Incorrect — mortgage lender consent is a contractual obligation, not a statutory pre-marketing requirement
Correct — current gas safety record must be obtained before each new tenancy
Correct — selective licence required if local authority has designated the area
Correct — EICR required every 5 years or at start of each new tenancy if sooner
questions.expl: Four statutory requirements before letting: EPC, Gas Safety Record (if gas), Selective licence (if applicable), EICR. Mortgage lender consent (B) is contractual, not a statutory pre-marketing requirement.
questions.key: Mortgage lender consent = contractual. EPC, Gas, EICR, Selective licence = statutory.
questions.ref: Energy Efficiency Regulations 2015; Gas Safety Regulations 1998; Electrical Safety Standards PRS 2020

questions.text: A tenant has lived in a property for three years. A valid Section 21 notice was served. The tenant refuses to leave at the end of the notice period. What is the MOST likely next step?
opts: The landlord can change the locks as the Section 21 notice period has expired
The landlord must apply to court for a possession order
The landlord can instruct private bailiffs immediately
The landlord must serve a second Section 21 notice before applying to court
optExpl: Incorrect — changing locks after S21 expiry is illegal eviction (criminal offence)
Correct — landlord must apply to court for a possession order; only court bailiffs can enforce it
Incorrect — private bailiffs have no authority without a court order
Incorrect — no second notice required; proceed to court on original S21
questions.expl: A Section 21 notice creates a right to apply to court — it does not automatically end the tenancy. After the notice period expires and the tenant remains, the landlord must apply to county court for a possession order, then apply for a warrant of possession.
questions.key: S21 → apply to court → possession order → warrant → court bailiffs. Each step requires the previous one.
questions.ref: Housing Act 1988, s.21; Protection from Eviction Act 1977; CPR Part 55

questions.text: Indicate whether the following information is needed before visiting a property, or can be gathered during the visit.

rows.left: Who the legal owner of the property is
The condition of the kitchen and bathrooms
rows.correct: Can be gathered during the visit

rows.left: Contact details for the person to be met
The number of bedrooms and approximate floor area
The landlord's desired or expected rent
rows.correct: Can be gathered during the visit

rows.left: Whether the property is freehold or leasehold
rows.correct: Can be gathered during the visit

cats: Can be gathered during the visit
questions.expl:
• Legal ownership: verify before visiting
• Contact details: needed to arrange the appointment
• Size/bedrooms: needed to find comparable evidence in advance
• Internal condition: assessed on arrival
• Landlord's expected rent: discussed at meeting
• Freehold/leasehold: confirmed during visit
questions.key: Before = verify ownership + logistics + gather comparables. During = condition, discussion, tenure details.

questions.text: Which statements about the referencing process for tenants and guarantors are correct? (Select all that apply.)
opts: The guarantor should be referenced using the same process as the tenant
The agent may waive guarantor referencing if the tenant has an excellent credit score
The referencing process should be set out in the agent's terms of business
An affordability check is standard, particularly where rent protection insurance is required
If a tenant fails referencing, the agent must reject their application immediately
optExpl: Correct — guarantor takes on tenant's obligations; must undergo same scrutiny
Incorrect — strong tenant does not exempt guarantor from referencing
Correct — terms of business should set out the referencing process
Correct — affordability assessment standard for rent guarantee insurance
Incorrect — failed referencing triggers a discussion with landlord about options
questions.expl: A guarantor must be properly vetted — waiving this (B) undermines the guarantee entirely. Immediate rejection for failed referencing (E) is not mandatory; alternatives can be discussed.
questions.key: Guarantor = same referencing as tenant. Strong tenant does NOT exempt guarantor.

questions.text: Fill in the blanks about Section 8 grounds for possession.

blanks.sentence: Ground 8 is a ___ ground, meaning the court must grant possession if proven. It requires at least ___ months' rent arrears at both the date of service and the date of the court hearing.
opts: mandatory
discretionary
two
three

blanks.sentence: Ground 10 applies where ___ rent is outstanding at service. Ground 11 can be used where the tenant has ___ been late paying rent, even if no arrears are currently outstanding.
opts: some
no
persistently
occasionally

questions.expl:
• Ground 8 (mandatory): court has no discretion — must grant possession if 2 months' arrears proven at service AND hearing
• Ground 10: some arrears at service; discretionary
• Ground 11: persistent late payment; discretionary; no current arrears needed
questions.key: G8 = mandatory, 2 months at service AND hearing. G10 = some arrears. G11 = persistent lateness.

questions.text: Under a fully managed contract, an agent prepares a detailed deposit claim at end of tenancy and charges the landlord an additional fee. Which BEST describes whether this is appropriate?
opts: Not appropriate — deposit claim preparation is always included in fully managed service
Appropriate — it is typically an additional service beyond the standard managed fee
Only appropriate if disclosed in the terms of business
Both B and C — the charge is appropriate as an extra service AND must have been disclosed in the ToB
optExpl: Incorrect — deposit claim preparation is not standard in most fully managed contracts
Partially correct but incomplete — pre-disclosure also required
Partially correct but incomplete — the nature of the work also matters
Correct — both: (1) legitimate additional service, AND (2) must be pre-disclosed in ToB
questions.expl: Two conditions must both be met: (1) deposit claim is legitimately chargeable as an additional service; (2) it must be clearly disclosed in the Terms of Business beforehand.
questions.key: Extra service + pre-disclosed in ToB = both conditions must be satisfied.
questions.ref: Consumer Rights Act 2015; Letting Agents (Transparency of Fees) Regulations 2015

questions.text: A local authority identifies a Category 1 hazard (severe damp) and a Category 2 hazard (loose handrail) in the same inspection. Which BEST describes the local authority's obligations?
opts: Must take appropriate action on Category 1 and has discretion on Category 2
Must act on both hazards as they were found in the same inspection
Can choose whether or not to act on either hazard
May only act on Category 2 if the tenant makes a separate complaint
optExpl: Correct — Category 1 triggers a duty; Category 2 gives a power
Incorrect — same inspection does not equalise the duty level
Incorrect — local authority has no discretion on Category 1
Incorrect — local authorities can act on Category 2 on their own initiative
questions.expl: HHSRS: Category 1 hazard → statutory duty to act. Category 2 hazard → power to act (discretionary).
questions.key: Category 1 = MUST act. Category 2 = MAY act. Always different obligations.
questions.ref: Housing Act 2004, ss.5–7 (Cat 1 duty), s.11 (Cat 2 power)

questions.text: Which statements about government-approved tenancy deposit schemes are correct? (Select all that apply.)
opts: In a custodial scheme, the scheme provider holds the deposit throughout the tenancy
In an insurance-backed scheme, the landlord or agent holds the deposit but pays a premium
Both types of scheme must be free of charge for the landlord to use
The landlord must provide prescribed information within 30 days of receiving the deposit
If held in a custodial scheme, the landlord does not need to provide prescribed information
optExpl: Correct — defining feature of custodial scheme
Correct — insurance-backed: landlord retains funds, pays premium
Incorrect — custodial is free; insurance-backed charges a premium to landlord/agent
Correct — prescribed information must be served within 30 days regardless of scheme type
Incorrect — prescribed information is always required, regardless of scheme type
questions.expl:
• Custodial: funds held by scheme; free to both parties
• Insurance-backed: funds held by landlord/agent; premium paid
• C wrong: insurance-backed charges landlord a premium
• E wrong: prescribed information always required
questions.key: ⚠️ Custodial = free to all. Insurance-backed = landlord pays premium. Prescribed info required in BOTH.

questions.text: Indicate whether the following describe a statutory periodic tenancy, a contractual periodic tenancy, or a new fixed-term tenancy.

rows.left: Created automatically by Section 5 HA 1988 when a fixed term expires and no action is taken
Arises where the original agreement contains a clause expressly providing for continuation
Requires both parties to sign a new written agreement setting out all terms afresh
The rent period mirrors the interval at which rent was paid under the original fixed term
Provides the greatest certainty for both parties about the duration going forward
Does not require the deposit to be re-protected under current regulations

questions.expl:
• Statutory periodic: automatic by law (s.5 HA 1988); rent period continues; deposit protection continues
• Contractual periodic: express clause in original tenancy
• New fixed-term: new signed agreement; most certainty
questions.key: Statutory = automatic by law. Contractual = in original agreement. New fixed-term = fresh contract.

questions.text: A tenant receives a Section 13 notice (£900→£1,080, 20% increase; local average 3%). Notice served by email without prior agreement. Which is NOT a valid basis to challenge the increase?
opts: The 20% increase far exceeds the local market average of 3%
The Section 13 notice was served by email without prior agreement
The tenant has already paid one month at the proposed new rent
The landlord did not give sufficient notice before the increase was due
optExpl: Valid challenge — FTT will compare against market evidence
Valid challenge — S13 notices must be served in prescribed form; electronic service without agreement may invalidate
NOT valid — paying new rent voluntarily = acceptance = FTT challenge right lost
Valid challenge — insufficient notice is a procedural defect
questions.expl: Options A, B and D are all valid challenges. Option C is the trap: paying the proposed new rent even once = voluntary acceptance = right to FTT challenge extinguished.
questions.key: ⚠️ Paying new rent (even once) = acceptance = FTT challenge right lost. Always challenge BEFORE paying.

questions.text: A landlord has ignored a roof leak for 3 months despite written notification. Water damage has spread to the tenant's bedroom. Which remedies are available to the tenant? (Select all that apply.)
opts: Report the disrepair to the local authority who may serve an improvement notice
Withhold all further rent payments until the landlord carries out the repair
Sue the landlord in court for damages caused by the disrepair
Carry out the repair and deduct the cost from rent without further notice to the landlord
Apply to court for an order requiring the landlord to carry out the repair
optExpl: Correct — local authority can inspect under HHSRS and serve improvement notice
Incorrect — withholding rent is not a recognised legal remedy; puts tenant at risk of rent arrears claim
Correct — civil claim for damages under LTA 1985 and/or Homes (Fitness for Human Habitation) Act 2018
Incorrect — repair and deduct is high-risk without proper legal steps
Correct — specific performance order is a recognised remedy
questions.expl:
• A: local authority referral, effective and common
• B: withholding rent = WRONG in law
• C: court claim for damages; appropriate
• D: repair and deduct = high-risk
• E: specific performance order; available
questions.key: ⚠️ Withholding rent = ALWAYS wrong in law. Report to council or sue = correct remedies.
questions.ref: LTA 1985 s.11; Homes (Fitness for Human Habitation) Act 2018; Housing Act 2004
